answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation* (Vol. I, ch. 6 and 9) both form part of his broader critique of deductive "systems" for founding civil/political laws and morality (chs. 5–9), contrasting them with his preferred analytical method: observing facts of human nature, needs, and consequences rather than conventions, duties, or dogmas.[^1] Chapter 6 targets Rousseau's social contract as a false origin for *lois civiles et politiques* (civil and political laws), which posits them as emerging from a primitive pact that magically instills morality where none existed in the "state of nature" (no justice, unlimited right to seize what one can attain).[^2] Comte rejects this as incoherent: it implies civil laws create morality ex nihilo via supposition, yet violation dissolves all obligations, reverting society to amoral force; true rights/duties preexist contracts, arising from natural relations (e.g., parent-child, labor on things) independent of conventions.[^3] Civil law here is framed as positive rules on property, persons, and powers, but wrongly detached from human faculties/organization.**

**Chapter 9 shifts to Jeremy Bentham's "principle of utility" (or well-understood interest), which explicitly *founds both morality and legislation* on maximizing good/minimizing evil for the human race.[^4] Comte endorses judging actions/laws by their aggregate effects (pleasures/pains) on humanity's progress—not individuals, nations, or castes—as this aligns with natural human sentiments (gratitude/hatred scaled by proximity/scale).[^5] Yet he separates *method*: science (legislation/morality as fact-study) expounds effects without prescribing duties ("public happiness *ought* to be the object"); rules/duties belong to art (governance/jurisprudence). Bentham errs by starting with utility as a moral imperative, risking controversy; facts alone compel assent.[^6]**

**Interaction: The chapters converge in rejecting non-factual bases (contract in ch. 6; duty/utility as axiom in ch. 9), rooting both civil law and morality in empirical consequences of human actions/institutions toward societal flourishing—effectively *folding* morality into civil law as shared effects of the "laws to which men are subjected by their nature" (foreshadowed in ch. 5, detailed later).[^7] Comte portrays civil laws (property/contracts enforced by state) as continuous with moral "laws" (habits/opinions enforced by social pains), both analyzable via utility to the race; false systems like Rousseau's artificially sever them, enabling despotism or vice.[^8]**

**Distinctions: Comte keeps them *apart* by domain and force—civil law as governmental commands with physical sanctions (chs. 11ff.); morality as non-coercive (social disapproval, later "moral pains" in ch. 16, "laws of morality" in chs. 18/21).[^9] Science treats both factually but doesn't conflate: legislation observes civil effects (e.g., contract violations → anarchy per Rousseau); morality observes habitual sentiments without imposing utility dogmatically.[^10] Civil law integrates moral facts (e.g., utility judgments underpin rights), but remains distinct as "power composed of elements" (physical/moral forces, chs. 14–20).**

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, ch. 2 (/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/02-de-la-methode-analytique-appliquee-a-l-etude-de-la/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., ch. 6, [p-2741b0](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/06-du-systeme-dans-lequel-on-considere-les-lois-civil/translation/#p-2741b0).

[^3]: Ibid., ch. 6, [p-008c9d](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/06-du-systeme-dans-lequel-on-considere-les-lois-civil/translation/#p-008c9d) and [p-a5bd7c](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/06-du-systeme-dans-lequel-on-considere-les-lois-civil/translation/#p-a5bd7c).

[^4]: Ibid., ch. 9 (/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/09-de-la-doctrine-qui-fonde-la-morale-et-la-legislati/translation/).

[^5]: Ibid., ch. 9, [p-5ad19d](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/09-de-la-doctrine-qui-fonde-la-morale-et-la-legislati/translation/#p-5ad19d).

[^6]: Ibid., ch. 9, [p-45fc4e](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/09-de-la-doctrine-qui-fonde-la-morale-et-la-legislati/translation/#p-45fc4e).

[^7]: Ibid., ch. 5 (/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/05-des-lois-auxquelles-les-hommes-sont-assujettis-par/translation/).

[^8]: Ibid., ch. 6, [p-3ef477](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/06-du-systeme-dans-lequel-on-considere-les-lois-civil/translation/#p-3ef477).

[^9]: Ibid., chs. 16 and 18 (/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/16-des-peines-et-des-plaisirs-moraux-consideres-comme/translation/; /works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/18-des-elements-de-puissance-dont-se-composent-les-lo/translation/).

[^10]: Ibid., ch. 9, [p-dd557a](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/09-de-la-doctrine-qui-fonde-la-morale-et-la-legislati/translation/#p-dd557a).

**Note:** Paragraph links derived from chapter texts; full corpus lacks exhaustive cross-references between chs. 6/9, but shared themes emerge via analytical method.[^1]
